source: Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic 1675-1740 (OUP 1986), passage-time tables: ~5-7 weeks via Madeira and the NE trades. NOT LIVE-VERIFIED in this session.
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic 1675-1740: post-1702 West India packet boats averaged about five weeks outward.
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic 1675-1740: westbound northern crossings averaged ~7-9 weeks against the westerlies.
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic 1675-1740; westbound times comparable to Boston, slightly longer on average.
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic 1675-1740: eastbound with the westerlies and Gulf Stream, ~4-5 weeks; fast passages under 3 weeks recorded.
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic 1675-1740: outward via Barbados and the Windward Passage, ~8-10 weeks.
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic 1675-1740: homeward beat through the Florida Strait, past Bermuda toward the Azores; ~10-11 weeks typical.
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic 1675-1740: North American provisioning trade to the West Indies, ~3-4 weeks southbound.
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic 1675-1740, intercolonial passage data.
Pierre & Huguette Chaunu, Seville et l'Atlantique (1955-59); A. Garcia-Baquero, Cadiz y el Atlantico 1717-1778: outward flota ~2.5-3.5 months via Canaries, Dominica passage, and Gulf of Mexico.
Chaunu, Seville et l'Atlantique; Carla Rahn Phillips, Six Galleons for the King of Spain: Tierra Firme galeones track via Canaries and the Antilles.
Chaunu, Seville et l'Atlantique; Garcia-Baquero: homeward via the Bahama Channel, Bermuda latitude, and the Azores.
C.R. Boxer, The Golden Age of Brazil 1695-1750 (1962): Brazil fleets typically ~2-3 months outward via Madeira/Cape Verde.
C.R. Boxer, The Golden Age of Brazil 1695-1750: Rio passage somewhat longer than Bahia, crossing the doldrums and SE trades.
David Richardson, Bristol, Africa and the Eighteenth-Century Slave Trade; Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database (slavevoyages.org): first leg ~6-8 weeks.
Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database, slavevoyages.org: early-18th-century Middle Passage means ~2 months from the Gold Coast; Barbados the shortest Caribbean landfall.
Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database, slavevoyages.org: Bight of Benin departures took longer (working off the Guinea coast), Jamaica deeper into the Caribbean than Barbados.
J.R. Bruijn, F.S. Gaastra & I. Schoffer, Dutch-Asiatic Shipping in the 17th and 18th Centuries (1987): VOC outward leg to the Cape averaged ~4 months in this period.

to: Batavia (via Brouwer route, roaring forties and Sunda Strait)
source: Bruijn, Gaastra & Schoffer, Dutch-Asiatic Shipping: post-1617 mandated Brouwer route, running east in ~36-42 S before turning north to Sunda Strait.
Bruijn, Gaastra & Schoffer, Dutch-Asiatic Shipping: full outward voyage averaged ~8 months in the early 18th century including the Cape refreshment stop.

K.N. Chaudhuri, The Trading World of Asia and the English East India Company 1660-1760 (1978); Anthony Farrington, Catalogue of East India Company Ships' Journals: ~5-6 months, timed to the SW monsoon.
K.N. Chaudhuri, The Trading World of Asia and the English East India Company 1660-1760; Farrington EIC journals.
William Lytle Schurz, The Manila Galleon (1939): downwind run of ~8-12 weeks via the Marianas.

to: Acapulco (eastbound galleon via Kuroshio and ~35-40 N)
source: William Lytle Schurz, The Manila Galleon: the dreaded return averaged ~6 months, sometimes 7-8; the longest regular non-stop passage in the age of sail.
